title: Deployed trusted-node quantum key distribution over 300 km with a multi-core fiber access link
authors: Martin Clason, Joakim Argillander, Didrik Bergström, Daniel Spegel-Lexne, Giulio Foletto, Ashraf El Hassan, Mohamed Bourennane, Onur Günlü, Katia Gallo, Rui Lin, Guilherme B. Xavier
abstract: Quantum key distribution (QKD) is increasingly considered for deployment in realistic communication networks, where long distances, heterogeneous fiber infrastructure, and coexistence with classical traffic present substantial challenges. Here, we demonstrate trusted-node QKD between Linköping University and the Stockholm hub of the Swedish national quantum communication infrastructure over 270 km of deployed single-mode fiber, extended by a 33 km multi-core fiber (MCF) segment emulating a metropolitan access link, for a total distance of 303 km. The two sub-links use commercial QKD systems whose receivers are interfaced with external superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors, enabling operation at losses beyond those supported by standard internal gated-mode detectors. We operate the link while actively switching the QKD channel between two MCF cores, with co-propagating Ethernet traffic and injected broadband optical noise in the other cores. The results demonstrate the integration of commercial QKD into demanding, dynamically reconfigurable fiber infrastructure relevant to future hybrid quantum-classical networks. Finally, using the generated secret keys, we illustrate how limited and time-varying QKD throughput affects one-time-pad-protected image transmission: image fidelity depends strongly on the available QKD-generated key budget and the choice of compression algorithm, highlighting application-level challenges for QKD-based encryption in realistic scenarios.
summary: The research demonstrates a functional trusted-node QKD link over 300 km using deployed commercial hardware integrated with multi-core fibers. Key findings include the necessity of advanced detectors (SNSPDs) for high performance and showing that the achievable key rate directly limits the fidelity of subsequent quantum-secured applications, such as image encryption.
main_problem: Demonstrating trusted-node Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) over long distances ($\sim 300$ km) in realistic communication networks that include heterogeneous fiber infrastructure and coexisting classical traffic.
main_result: The setup successfully demonstrates QKD operation with active core switching and autonomous reinitialization in a dynamic, multi-core fiber environment, while also showing how image fidelity depends on the available key budget.
method: The authors implemented a trusted-node QKD link using commercial systems interfaced with external superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) over deployed SMF and MCFs.
model_or_system: The physical system is a deployed quantum communication network spanning $\sim 303$ km, utilizing two sub-links connected by a trusted node. The LiU end incorporates a $33 	ext{ km}$ Multi-Core Fiber (MCF) to simulate metropolitan access links coexisting with classical Ethernet traffic and optical noise.
key_observables: Secret Key Rate ($	ext{SKR}$), Quantum Bit Error Rate ($	ext{QBER}$), Image Fidelity (PSNR, LPIPS).
important_parameters: Total distance of $\sim 303 	ext{ km}$; SNSPD detection efficiency and dark count rates; key budget size in kbits.
main_assumptions: The system relies on Key Management Systems ($	ext{KMSs}$) buffers to smooth out short-term fluctuations in end-to-end key generation rate, and the classical link routing does not require symmetry.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup map, $	ext{SKR}$ and $	ext{QBER}$ measurements under varying noise levels (including SNSPD cycle effects), and a comparison of image reconstruction fidelity using JPEG 2000 versus deep-learning compression (JPEG AI) as a function of available key budget.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from characterizing the physical link setup (long-distance trusted node over SMF/MCF) to demonstrating robust operation under dynamic noise conditions, culminating in an application study showing how limited QKD throughput constrains high-level tasks like image encryption.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it bridges fundamental quantum communication theory with practical engineering challenges, specifically addressing the integration of quantum signals into complex, noisy, and dynamically reconfigurable classical fiber infrastructure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Overview of the deployed experiment in southeastern Sweden. We deploy a long-distance QKD link between Link¨oping University (LiU) and the the NQCIS Albanova hub at KTH in Stockholm. The two end nodes are connected to an intermediate trusted node located outside of Nyk¨oping (NYK) through deployed single-mode fibers (SMFs). Within the LiU node an additional 33 km multi-core fiber (MCF) spool is employed extending the NYK-LiU link, while allowing spatial multiplexing of classical data channels and additional broadband noise simulating a real populated link. Both QKD receivers (Bob) have been modified to support external superconducting nanowire single-photon detection (SNSPD)...

Figure 3. QKD field-trial with active spatial multiplexing in a MCF access link. a) SKR for the Link¨oping-Nyk¨oping link, with the QKD channel passing through either core 1 or 6 of the MCF, with the other core carrying the downstream channel of a 10 Gbit/s Ethernet optical connection. The upstream channel is fixed at core 7. The other four cores carry broadband noise generated from an LED, whose current we gradually increase within each core cycle, as shown in the figure. b) Reported quantum bit error rate (QBER) for the two measurement bases (X and Z). Figure 4. SKR performance and information-theoretically secure image transmission. (a) Impact of injected broad- band noise from the LED on the deployed long-distance QKD channel connected to the MCF access link. The error bars represent the standard deviation of the SKR distribution for each LED current. (b) We use the available secret key bits to encrypt the compressed reference image using OTP, given the generated key aggregated over a time budget of 100 s. Each image is compressed using both JPEG 2000 and JPEG AI, where we compare the decompression outputs by splitting each image in half and showing the visual results.
